[2018-03-22 10:14] steel0229e (Steel): I like the discussion about what type of learning is best and how it is all subjective
[2018-03-22 10:14] steel0229e (Steel): I am also a chess player and I found that to really improve at any game, I need to play slow and long games and think a lot about them
[2018-03-22 10:15] steel0229e (Steel): I am also against pushing new players into thinking fast and making them play blitz
[2018-03-22 10:15] steel0229e (Steel): on the other hand I am an RTS player as well and I also picked up the habit of grinding fast games to get good, so I try to mix things
[2018-03-22 10:18] steel0229e (Steel): I think the way colledcannon thinks explains exactly why he has hit a skill plateau. I don't believe you should focus on your instincts. I made that mistake in Prismata for a long time and I had to work really hard to get rid of bad habits and to not autopilot and slow down when a move seemed obvious. You have to always slow down and seriously think about stuff if you want to improve in my opinion.
[2018-03-22 10:19] steel0229e (Steel): Also I am not sure where my stance is with the grind vs study debate. I'd think studying is a lot more important than playing the game, but can be wrong. I think heuristics can mislead you because they are based on instinct again. It helps at short time controls, but can backfire when you have enough time to optimise.
[2018-03-22 10:22] steel0229e (Steel): My 2 cents is that if you want to maximise the efficiency of the time you spend on the game and and you want to optimise for max improvement/time spent, you should study. Playing 100 blitz games where you never analayse your mistakes and where you could havemade a better move is worth much less than 1 hour of deep study.
[2018-03-22 10:24] steel0229e (Steel): I have over 20k games played and I value that much less than the time I spent thinking about the game or analysing. Part of this is because I palyed a lot of bot games which contributed nothing to my skill exept seeing and thinking about a lot of different sets.
[2018-03-22 10:30] p0lari (Polari): the way I'd put it is the 100 blitz games in that comparison are good at developing your basic intuition on how different types of strategies play out at a broad level, but it's not going to lead to any deeper new insights
[2018-03-22 10:31] p0lari (Polari): and that that kind of experience is more efficient at getting you to maybe 1700-1800 level or so, at which point you'll need to start thinking deeper
[2018-03-22 10:32] p0lari (Polari): though of course even then it needs to be supplemented by *some* thinking about those basic concepts, if you literally just mash games doing the same thing over and over without thinking about it, you're not going to improve regardless of what level you're at

messages.content: I am also against pushing new players into thinking fast and making them play blitz

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: on the other hand I am an RTS player as well and I also picked up the habit of grinding fast games to get good, so I try to mix things

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: I think the way colledcannon thinks explains exactly why he has hit a skill plateau. I don't believe you should focus on your instincts. I made that mistake in Prismata for a long time and I had to work really hard to get rid of bad habits and to not autopilot and slow down when a move seemed obvious. You have to always slow down and seriously think about stuff if you want to improve in my opinion.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: Also I am not sure where my stance is with the grind vs study debate. I'd think studying is a lot more important than playing the game, but can be wrong. I think heuristics can mislead you because they are based on instinct again. It helps at short time controls, but can backfire when you have enough time to optimise.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: My 2 cents is that if you want to maximise the efficiency of the time you spend on the game and and you want to optimise for max improvement/time spent, you should study. Playing 100 blitz games where you never analayse your mistakes and where you could havemade a better move is worth much less than 1 hour of deep study.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: I have over 20k games played and I value that much less than the time I spent thinking about the game or analysing. Part of this is because I palyed a lot of bot games which contributed nothing to my skill exept seeing and thinking about a lot of different sets.

messages.author: p0lari
messages.content: the way I'd put it is the 100 blitz games in that comparison are good at developing your basic intuition on how different types of strategies play out at a broad level, but it's not going to lead to any deeper new insights

messages.author: p0lari
messages.content: and that that kind of experience is more efficient at getting you to maybe 1700-1800 level or so, at which point you'll need to start thinking deeper

messages.author: p0lari
messages.content: though of course even then it needs to be supplemented by *some* thinking about those basic concepts, if you literally just mash games doing the same thing over and over without thinking about it, you're not going to improve regardless of what level you're at
